meta.name: Spaghetti alla Carbonara
meta.subtitle: Egg-and-cheese emulsion with cured pork and black pepper (no cream)

source.name: ssa-core
notes: Canonical Roman-style carbonara: eggs + Pecorino Romano (or Pecorino/Parm blend), guanciale (or pancetta), and lots of black pepper.
No cream. Sauce forms by emulsifying egg/cheese with hot pasta water off heat.
Best eaten immediately; reheating can scramble eggs.
If you must reheat, use low heat + splash of water, stirring constantly.
Saltiness comes from cheese + cured pork; go easy on salting the pasta water.
Black pepper should be bold and aromatic.

optional.label: Instant-read thermometer
optional.notes: Helpful to keep sauce zone ~65–75°C / 150–170°F (hot enough to thicken, not scramble).

ingredients.label: Guanciale, cut into lardons
ingredients.notes: Best choice. Substitute pancetta if needed.

ingredients.label: Egg yolks
ingredients.notes: Extra yolks make the sauce richer and more stable.

ingredients.label: Pecorino Romano, finely grated
ingredients.notes: Option: blend with Parmigiano-Reggiano if you want it slightly less sharp.

ingredients.label: Freshly ground black pepper
ingredients.notes: Use more to taste; pepper is signature.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt (for pasta water)
ingredients.notes: Adjust based on saltiness of cheese/pork.

ingredients.label: Reserved pasta cooking water
ingredients.notes: You may use 60–180 ml (1/4–3/4 cup) depending on consistency.

steps.title: Make the egg-cheese base
instructions: In a mixing bowl, whisk together whole eggs and yolks until smooth.
Whisk in most of the grated Pecorino (reserve a handful for serving).
Add a generous amount of freshly ground black pepper.
checks: Mixture is thick and cohesive (paste-like is okay).

steps.title: Render the guanciale
instructions: Place guanciale in a cold skillet and set over medium-low heat.
Cook slowly until fat renders and pieces are crisp-golden, 8–12 minutes.
Turn off heat. Keep the rendered fat in the pan.
checks: Guanciale is crisp on edges, not burnt.
Pan has a pool of rendered fat.

steps.title: Cook pasta and reserve water
instructions: Bring a large pot of water to a boil; salt it (moderately).
Cook spaghetti until just al dente (about 1–2 minutes shy of package time).
Before draining, reserve at least 1 cup (240 ml) of starchy pasta water.

steps.title: Emulsify into a glossy sauce (off heat)
instructions: Add drained pasta to the skillet with guanciale and rendered fat (heat OFF). Toss to coat.
Let the pasta cool for 30–60 seconds (important: too hot can scramble eggs).
Add a small splash of pasta water (2–3 tbsp) and toss.
Pour in the egg-cheese mixture while tossing constantly with tongs.
Add pasta water a little at a time until the sauce turns glossy and clings to the pasta.
checks: Sauce is glossy and creamy, not clumpy.

steps.title: Finish and serve
instructions: Taste and adjust with more black pepper and a little more cheese if desired.
Serve immediately with reserved Pecorino on top and extra pepper.
checks: Dish is hot, glossy, and creamy (no cream used).

notes: This recipe uses eggs that are gently thickened by hot pasta and residual heat. Use fresh, properly refrigerated eggs.
If serving to high-risk individuals (pregnant, immunocompromised), consider using pasteurized eggs.
Best eaten fresh; refrigerate leftovers up to 2 days.
Sauce will tighten in the fridge.
Reheat gently in a skillet over low heat with a splash of water, stirring constantly.
Avoid microwaving on high (risk of curdling/scrambling).
No cream: the creamy texture comes from egg + cheese + starchy water emulsion.
If your sauce is too thick, add more hot pasta water 1 tbsp at a time.
If sauce looks thin, toss longer; it thickens as the eggs gently set.

variations.label: Use pancetta if guanciale is unavailable

notes: Swap equal weight pancetta. It’s slightly less rich; add a small drizzle of olive oil if pan seems dry.
Use 50 g Pecorino + 30 g Parmigiano-Reggiano for a rounder profile.
Bucatini holds sauce well; cook to al dente and emulsify the same way.
A bright salad balances the richness.
Keep sides simple; carbonara is the star.
Prep: grate cheese and cut guanciale ahead.
Cook pasta and emulsify right before serving.
